summary_text:
Chief Complaint
Right hand numbness and tingling, primarily affecting the thumb, index, and middle fingers.

History of Present Illness
The patient reports experiencing numbness and tingling in his right hand for approximately six months, with the symptoms becoming more persistent over the past month. The numbness and tingling are most prominent when typing on the keyboard, particularly with the pain being exacerbated when gripping the keyboard. Ibuprofen and avoiding keyboard use provide some relief. Pain is also experienced with wrist extension and flexion, and when moving the wrist. The patient denies any recent trauma or other neurological symptoms. He reports no nighttime awakenings due to the symptoms.

Findings
Physical examination reveals tenderness to palpation in the right wrist. Grip strength is reduced on the right hand. Tinel's sign is positive over the right wrist. The patient grimaces when the wrist is tapped, indicating pain. Examination reveals pain on wrist extension and flexion.

Assessment
Based on the patient's symptoms, physical examination findings, and the positive Tinel's sign, carpal tunnel syndrome is the most likely diagnosis.

Plan
I am prescribing a wrist splint to wear at night and continue with ibuprofen up to 600 milligrams four times daily. I am ordering an EMG to further evaluate the nerve conduction studies in the right wrist. We will follow up in four weeks to assess response to treatment and discuss the possibility of physical therapy or surgery if conservative management is unsuccessful. } />.  600 milligrams four times a day and We will follow up in four weeks to assess response to treatment and discuss the possibility of physical therapy or surgery if conservative management is unsuccessful. } />.  600 milligrams four times a day and We will follow up in four weeks to assess response to treatment and discuss the possibility of physical therapy or surgery if conservative management is unsuccessful. } />.  600 milligrams four times a day and We will follow up in four weeks to assess response to treatment and discuss the possibility of physical therapy or surgery if conservative management is unsuccessful. } />.  600 milligrams four times a day and We will follow up in four weeks to assess response to treatment and discuss the possibility of physical therapy or surgery if conservative management is unsuccessful. } />.  600 milligrams four times a day and We will follow up in four weeks to assess response to treatment and discuss the possibility of physical therapy or surgery if conservative management is unsuccessful. } />.  600 milligrams four times a day and We will follow up in four weeks to assess response to treatment and discuss the possibility of physical therapy or surgery if conservative management is unsuccessful. } />.  600 milligrams four times a day and We will follow up in four weeks to assess response to treatment and discuss the possibility of physical therapy or surgery if conservative management is unsuccessful.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Right hand pain.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Alexis Gutierrez is a pleasant 33-year-old female who presents to the clinic today for the evaluation of right hand pain.

Approximately 6 months ago, the patient began experiencing intermittent episodes of numbness and tingling in the 1st, 2nd, and 3rd digits on her right hand. The numbness and tingling has become constant over the past month or so and is accompanied by pain with typing and using a mouse, which she does all day while working on a keyboard. She denies any sleep disturbance associated with her symptoms. Rest and ibuprofen have been the most helpful at providing symptomatic relief.

SOCIAL HISTORY

The patient reports that she works on a keyboard all day.

MEDICATIONS

The patient reports that she has been taking ibuprofen.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Denies sleep disturbance.
Musculoskeletal: Reports pain in the 1st, 2nd, and 3rd digits on the right hand.
Neurological: Reports numbness and tingling in the 1st, 2nd, and 3rd digits on the right hand.

PHYSICAL EXAM

MSK: Examination of the right hand: Pain with flexion and extension of the wrist.. Decreased grip strength on the right compared to the left. Positive Tinel's sign.

ASSESSMENT

Right carpal tunnel syndrome.

PLAN

After reviewing her symptoms and exam findings, I believe her symptoms are secondary to carpal tunnel syndrome. We discussed the nature of this condition, as well as her treatment options, in detail. I recommend we treat this conservatively with nighttime wrist splinting and ibuprofen, but I want to increase her dose to 600 mg 4 times daily. I'm also going to order an EMG/Nerve conduction study for further evaluation. If this is positive, we can consider additional treatment with physical therapy or surgical intervention with carpal tunnel release.

section_miscategorizations.reason: The clinical content (ibuprofen dosing and follow-up plan) is correctly placed in the Plan section, but it is duplicated multiple times with embedded markup artifacts. This is an organizational/formatting issue rather than a true section miscategorization and does not substantially distort the clinical meaning.
